title: Spectral gaps without frustration
speaker: Marius Lemm
affiliation: California Institute of Technology; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: In spin systems, the existence of a spectral gap has far-reaching consequences. So-called "frustration-free" spin systems form a subclass that is special enough to make the spectral gap problem amenable and, at the same time, broad enough to include physically relevant examples. We discuss "finite-size criteria", which allow to bound the spectral gap of the infinite system by the spectral gap of finite subsystems. We focus on the connection between spectral gaps and boundary conditions. Joint work with E. Mozgunov.

proof_stages.label: Projection spin-chain Hamiltonian and gap dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a one-dimensional nearest-neighbor quantum spin chain with Hilbert space tensor powers of C^d, Hamiltonian H_m built from a single local projection p, and spectral gap gamma_m, then formulates the uniform gapped versus gapless dichotomy as m grows.

proof_stages.label: Motivation for the gap problem and frustration-freeness
proof_stages.summary: Spectral gaps are motivated by control of ground states, correlations, area laws, and phase transitions, while the general gap decision problem is hard, so the talk introduces frustration-freeness as the existence of a common zero-energy state satisfying all local projection constraints.

proof_stages.label: Scope and checkability of the frustration-free regime
proof_stages.summary: The frustration-free assumption is shown to be non-vacuous via rank conditions and standard models, and later the role of translation-invariant or uniformly controlled local interactions is clarified for the finite-size bounds.

proof_stages.label: Periodic finite-size criterion and boundary asymmetry
proof_stages.summary: The previous Knabe/Gosset–Mozgunov criterion lower-bounds the periodic-chain gap gamma_m^per by a finite open-chain gap gamma_n minus an explicit threshold, proving open-gapped implies periodic-gapped but leaving the converse obstructed by boundary effects.

proof_stages.label: Open-chain finite-size criterion
proof_stages.summary: The new result replaces the periodic gap on the left by the open-chain gap gamma_m, at the cost of using a minimum over comparable subsystem gaps and a threshold of order n^{-3/2}, giving a checkable finite-size sufficient condition for the open chain to be uniformly gapped.

proof_stages.label: Consequences of the n^{-3/2} threshold and higher-dimensional edge modes
proof_stages.summary: The contrapositive says that a gapless frustration-free chain must have sufficiently small subsystem gaps in dyadic windows, excluding slower finite-size closings such as 1/n; this is interpreted as ruling out massless edge modes and is extended in two dimensions using coarse graining and rhomboid-shaped subsystems.

proof_stages.label: Frustration-free square-inequality reduction
proof_stages.summary: The proof begins by using frustration-freeness to identify the kernel with the zero-energy ground space, making the spectral-gap lower bound H_m >= c on the orthogonal complement equivalent to the operator inequality H_m^2 >= c H_m on the whole space.

proof_stages.label: Knabe subsystem-square estimate
proof_stages.summary: Expanding H^2 separates positive distant commuting terms from dangerous overlapping anticommutators, and sums of squared small-subsystem Hamiltonians bound these anticommutators in terms of finite subsystem gaps, yielding the finite-size criterion in the basic periodic case.

examples.name: Heisenberg symmetric/antisymmetric projections and frustration intuition

examples.engagement_reason: The Haldane gap problem is cited as a famous long-open conjecture illustrating difficulty of gap questions.

examples.name: Undecidable spectral-gap Hamiltonians
examples.note: The undecidability result for translation-invariant quantum Hamiltonians illustrates the algorithmic hardness that motivates imposing frustration-freeness.
examples.engagement_reason: Undecidable spectral-gap Hamiltonians are described at the level of the known undecidability result and translation-invariance/frustration-free status, without constructing or analyzing one.

passes.reason: The undecidable spectral-gap Hamiltonian constructions are not structural classification exceptions, and they obstruct any general algorithm for deciding gaps.

examples.name: Boundary-induced gaplessness with periodic bulk gap
examples.note: The speaker invokes two-dimensional examples where open boundaries support low-energy states while the periodic bulk remains gapped, illustrating why the periodic criterion cannot simply be reversed.
examples.engagement_reason: Boundary-induced gaplessness with a periodic bulk gap is described qualitatively as an existing two-dimensional phenomenon, without a concrete model or calculation.

passes.reason: The boundary-gapless but periodic-gapped systems are structurally ordinary, but explicitly refute the converse from periodic bulk gap to open-boundary gap.

examples.name: Massless edge quasiparticle on a finite cylinder

examples.engagement_reason: The finite-cylinder edge quasiparticle is worked through heuristically by imposing discrete momenta and computing from the linear dispersion that the finite-size gap scales like order 1/n.
